primary_diagnosis.name: Temporomandibular disorder — acute masseter/temporalis myofascial spasm (trismus) with central sensitisation

pathophysiology_summary: Temporomandibular disorder with bruxism causes masseter/temporalis myofascial spasm producing acute trismus, TMJ pain and referred temporal/neck pain, amplified by fibromyalgia-type central sensitisation. The alarming trismus mimics a deep-space dental infection (Ludwig-type, airway-threatening), giant cell arteritis (jaw claudication) and tetanus. Reassuring features are bilateral muscular spasm without submandibular/floor-of-mouth swelling, drooling or airway compromise; non-tender temporal arteries with no scalp tenderness/jaw-claudication/visual change (and a young age); no focal dental abscess/fever; up-to-date tetanus with no descending rigidity; and a jaw stuck closed by spasm (not fixed dislocated open). However, telehealth cannot examine the mouth/masseters/temporal arteries or measure jaw opening, and trismus limits intake with dehydration risk. The correct action is urgent in-person dental/GP review plus supportive care (heat, soft/liquid diet, simple analgesia, gentle jaw rest) — not remote benzodiazepines/tricyclics — with 000/urgent care for neck/chin swelling with breathing difficulty, sudden vision loss/GCA features, or a jaw stuck open.

differential.diagnosis: TMD myofascial spasm with central sensitisation
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral masseter spasm/trismus after bruxism on a fibromyalgia background.
Trismus can accompany deep-space infection; screen neck/chin swelling, drooling, breathing.
Screen scalp tenderness/jaw claudication/visual change (less likely at this age).
Trismus is a tetanus sign; screen vaccination and generalized rigidity.

differential.diagnosis: TMD myofascial spasm with central sensitisation
differential.evidence_basis: No airway/deep-space-infection features, no GCA features, tetanus screen negative, jaw stuck closed — myofascial TMD.
Lower probability but not excludable without an intra-oral/neck exam.
No cranial ischaemic features; young age.

differential.diagnosis: TMD myofascial spasm with central sensitisation
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: myofascial TMD/trismus needing urgent in-person dental/GP review, supportive care, hydration, and airway/GCA/dislocation safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Swelling under the chin/floor of mouth/neck, drooling, or difficulty breathing/swallowing saliva (deep-space infection/airway)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ludwig-type infection can obstruct the airway — an emergency.

red_flags.description: New scalp tenderness/jaw claudication on chewing, or any visual change (GCA)
red_flags.clinical_significance: GCA is vision-threatening (less likely at this age but must be screened).

red_flags.description: Focal dental abscess with high fever, or generalized descending rigidity/spasms (tetanus)
Jaw fixed dislocated (stuck open) or inability to maintain fluids (dehydration)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A fixed open dislocation needs reduction; trismus with poor intake risks dehydration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening submandibular/neck swelling, drooling and breathing in acute trismus.
Prescribing diazepam or starting amitriptyline over telehealth.
Not addressing reduced intake from painful mouth opening.
Either missing GCA if features are present, or over-investigating a young patient with no cranial features.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: No airway/deep-space-infection features, no GCA features, jaw stuck closed — myofascial TMD
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports urgent in-person dental/GP review with supportive care and airway/GCA safety-netting.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen airway/deep-space-infection, GCA and dislocation; advise heat, soft/liquid diet, hydration and simple analgesia; arrange urgent in-person dental/GP review; escalate to 000 for neck/chin swelling with breathing difficulty, sudden vision loss, or a jaw stuck open.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing acute trismus to TMD without screening a deep-space infection/airway could miss a Ludwig-type emergency; missing GCA risks vision.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Oral and Dental (TMD/dental infection)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: TMD myofascial pain is managed with reassurance, jaw rest, soft diet, heat and simple analgesia; deep-space odontogenic infection with airway signs is an emergency.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP guidance on giant cell arteritis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Jaw claudication, scalp tenderness or visual change require urgent GCA assessment and steroids.
